coredata.dc:title: Lignin-derived graphene quantum dots from phosphous acid-assisted hydrothermal pretreatment and their application in photocatalysis
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Wei
Ning, Chenxi
Sang, Ranran
Hou, Qingxi
Ni, Yonghao
coredata.dc:description: Value- added utilization of carbon-rich lignin has become an urgent and attractive research topic due to its abundance, low cost, and sustainability. How to convert lignin in an efficient and green strategy is the bottleneck limiting its valorization. Herein, a phosphorous acid (P) assisted hydrothermal process to produce lignin-derived graphene quantum dots (P-LGQDs) was developed. The as-prepared P-LGQDs was comprehensively characterized, and explored in photocatalysis. The P-LGQDs have a narrow particle size distribution, excellent electronic and optical properties, and a good pH-responsiveness. The P-LGQDs was further used to construct P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunction, showing excellent photocatalytic degradation ability towards methylene blue and model lignin (94.6 % and 93.1 %, respectively). This present study offers a universal strategy for the value-added utilization and valorization of lignin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin
Graphene quantum dots
Photocatalysis
Hydrothermal pretreatment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence sensing of LGQDs at different pH values
pH responsiveness of LGQDs and P-LGQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to express their gratitude for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32071715 , 31770634 ), and National Science Foundation for Post-doctoral Scientists of China ( 2019M651050 ), and Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control, College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University ( 2019KF32 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image and particle size distribution of LGQDs (a) and P-LGQDs (b).

FTIR spectra of GQDs (a); UV–vis absorption spectra, photoluminescent Ex and Em spectra and UV–vis photos of GQDs (b); 3D fluorescence spectra of LGQDs (c) and P-LGQDs (d). Photoluminescence spectra of LGQDs (e) and P-LGQDs (f) at different excitation wavelengths.
XPS wide scan spectra of LGQDs and P-LGQDs (a); high resolution XPS N1s (b) core-level spectrum of LGQDs; high resolution XPS N1s (c), and P2p(d) core-level spectra of P-LGQDs.
pH responsiveness of LGQDs (a, c) and P-LGQDs (b, d).

FTIR spectra (a) of P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 and ultrasonic g-C3N4; N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (b) and pore size distribution curves (c) of P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 and ultrasonic g-C3N4; PL emission spectra (λex = 345 nm) (d), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (e), and band gap energy spectra (f) of P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 and ultrasonic g-C3N4, respectively; FESEM (g-1, h-1), TEM (g-2, h-2), and HRTEM (g-3, h-3) images of ultrasonic g-C3N4 (g) and P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 (h); element mapping images of P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 (i).
Photocatalytic degradation of MB (a), and mass spectrogram of photocatalytic degradation products from model lignin (b) with P-LGQDs/g-C3N4.
Proposed photocatalytic degradation Mechanism of P-LGQDs/g-C3N4.
Photocatalytic transformation of model lignin and yield with P-LGQDs/g-C3N4.

head.ce:title: Lignin-derived graphene quantum dots from phosphous acid-assisted hydrothermal pretreatment and their application in photocatalysis

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Pulp & Paper, Tianjin University of Science & Technology, Tianjin, 300457, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Pulp & Paper

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Chenxi Ning and Ranran Sang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Value- added utilization of carbon-rich lignin has become an urgent and attractive research topic due to its abundance, low cost, and sustainability. How to convert lignin in an efficient and green strategy is the bottleneck limiting its valorization. Herein, a phosphorous acid (P) assisted hydrothermal process to produce lignin-derived graphene quantum dots (P-LGQDs) was developed. The as-prepared P-LGQDs was comprehensively characterized, and explored in photocatalysis. The P-LGQDs have a narrow particle size distribution, excellent electronic and optical properties, and a good pH-responsiveness. The P-LGQDs was further used to construct P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunction, showing excellent photocatalytic degradation ability towards methylene blue and model lignin (94.6 % and 93.1 %, respectively). This present study offers a universal strategy for the value-added utilization and valorization of lignin.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy and environment are key issues in the 21st century. Bio- solutions to both are highly desirable. For this purpose, lignocellulosic biomass, mainly consisting of cellulose, hemicelluloses, and lignin, has received much attention due to its renewability and abundance. In addition to cellulose, lignin is also the most abundant natural polymer. Currently, great efforts have been dedicated to convert lignin to plastics, adhesives, elastomers, hydrogels, aerogels, nanoparticles and other functional polymeric materials (Gu et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020). Carbon-rich lignin is mainly composed of p-hydroxyphenyl units, guaiacyl units, and syringyl units, whose holistic utilization is of great importance (Ragauskas et al., 2014; Pei et al., 2020). So far, lignin has been studied to produce carbon-based materials such as porous carbon, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots, porous carbon spheres, carbon fibre, and carbon aerogels (Ding et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019a; Torres-Canas et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2015).

Graphene quantum dots, as one new generation of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, possess unique properties including good solubility, tunable photoluminescence, high electron mobility, biocompatibility, and easy functionalization (Lu et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2015), with various applications such as bio-imaging, environmental monitoring, chemical analysis, and catalyst (Yan et al., 2019). Carbon-rich lignin has been regarded as an excellent feedstock for fabricating carbon-based materials (McClelland et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019a), which is attractive because of their economic and environmental advantages. Ding et al. (2018) prepared single-crystalline graphene quantum dots from lignin through two-step method, and then explored as nanoprobes for multicolour bioimaging. Wang et al. (2019a) converted alkali lignin into S, N co-dopped GQDs, and applied as ensitive fluorescence-switchable probe for detecting H2O2. Nair et al. (2020) used lignosulfonate as carbon source to prepare metal- and nonmetal-codoped GQDs, and explored its application as fluorescent probes for detecting toxic metal ions in water. Furthermore, lignin-derived graphene quantum dots (LGQDs) offer excellent photoluminescence properties including strong fluorescence, good up-conversion ability, long-term photostability, and excellent biocompatibility (Wang et al., 2019a; Xu et al., 2020). So far, GQDs have attracted great attention to improve the photocatalytic performance of semiconductors. However, the potential application of LGQDs in the field of photocatalysis has nearly been explored.

As for the preparation of carbon-based materials, hydrothermal treatment has been reported as an effective approach since it has many advantages including energy saving, environmentally benign, and low corrosiveness (Wu et al., 2020). Lignin is three dimensional network with alpha- beta- aryl ether bonds as the main inter-phenylpropane linkages, cleavages of these ether bonds are expected to impart favorable morphological features to resultant carbon materials. In addition, doping nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and sulfur (S) elements into carbon can enhance its performance in catalysis, because heteroatoms can adjust the electron-donor properties of carbon materials, add more coordination sites, and introduce more defects, consequently tuning the electrical and chemical performance of their surface (Li et al., 2016b; Xu et al., 2018). Herein, the concept of phosphorous acid assisted hydrothermal treatment of lignin was proposed, with the hypotheses that 1) phosphorous acid can catalyze the cleavage of inter-aryl ether bonds, thus inducing more effective lignin degradation during the hydrothermal treatment; 2) phosphorous acid as phosphorus doping agent can form the substitutional defects in the diamond sp3 thin films, and as n-type donor to bring more P-containing functional groups, thereby changing electronic and optical properties of resultant GQD (Yang et al., 2018); furthermore, the added phosphorous acid also functions as phosphorus doping agent, which can change the electronic and optical properties, and generate new and unexpected properties of GQDs through changing their electronic properties and providing more available active sites.

In this study, we report a new strategy, i.e., a phosphorous acid (P) assisted hydrothermal process to produce lignin-derived graphene quantum dots (P-LGQDs). The as-prepared P-LGQDs was comprehensively characterized, and further explored as photocatalysts in lignin and dye model degradation. As far as we know, it is the first study to construct LGQDs-based photocatalysts for lignin degrading. This study provides a new approach for value-added utilization and potential applications of lignin-derived carbon materials in photocatalytic degradation.

The corncob lignin was provided by Shandong Longlive Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China. Corncob lignin was mainly composed of Klason lignin (90.81 %), acid soluble lignin (3.61 %), carbohydrates (0.63 %), ash (2.16 %), and others (2.79 %). The Mw, Mn, and Mw/Mn of corncob lignin were 3258 g/mol, 2260 g/mol, and 1.44, respectively (Yang et al., 2014). Melamine was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. 2-phenoxy-1-phenylethanol (C14H14O2) was purchased from Bide Pharmatech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 4'-methylacetophenone was obtained from Tianjin Yifang Science & Technology Co., Ltd., China. Sodium citrate was received from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Factory, China. Methylene blue (MB), phosphoric acid and sodium bicarbonate were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Sodium hydroxide was received from Tianjin Jiangtian Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. In this study, all chemicals are of analytical grade. Throughout the experiment, deionized water was used.

Fig. 1 illustrates the experimental flow of this study. 24.0 g of lignin was thoroughly mixed with 120 mL of deionized water. The mixture was added into an autoclave reactor (Model 4556, Parr Instrument Company, USA), heated to 180 °C and kept for 5 h. The reactor was naturally cooled down to room temperature after the reaction. Then, the mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter, the liquid and the solid parts were collected and stored for later use, respectively. The liquid parts were dialyzed with a semi-permeable dialysis tube with Mw cutoff of 3500 Da for 48 h, the deionized water was used and changed every 6 h during the period. The solution was freeze-dried to collect solid powders. The LGQDs were then obtained and regarded as the control. In addition, prior to hydrothermal treatment of corncob lignin, 24.0 g H3PO4 was mixed with the mixture including 24.0 g of lignin and 120 mL of deionized water, the preparation and conditions were consistent with that of the control. Thereby, the phosphorus (P)-doped lignin-derived graphene quantum dots were obtained, i.e., P-LGQDs.

g-C3N4 was prepared using previously reported methods (Tang et al., 2019) Briefly, 5.0 g melamine was put in a ceramic crucible, and heated at 550 °C for 4 h. The yellow powders g-C3N4 were obtained, and treated using ultrasonic waves at 300 W for 2 h to ensure the g-C3N4 was separated into layer-by-layer structures, obtaining the ultrasonic g-C3N4. Then, the P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunction was prepared by dipping method, i.e., 400 mg ultrasonic g-C3N4 and 4.0 mg P-LGQDs were dispersed in deionized water (20 mL), thoroughly mixed, and continually treated at an ultrasonic power of 300 W for 1 h at 30 °C. The mixture was freeze-dried to get the P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunction composites.

LGQDs were also comprehensively characterized. The surface functional groups were analyzed using FTIR spectrophotometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a DR-6000 UV–vis spectrophotometer (HACH, USA). Fluorescence spectra were determined with a HITACHI F-7100 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Ltd., Japan). TEM images were observed by using a JEM-F200 Cold FEG transmission electron microscopy at 200 kV. An Escalab 250Xi spectrometer equipped with a mono X-Ray source Al Kα excitation (1350.08 eV) was used to get XPS data.

The Tauc plot was used to get band gap of a semiconductor. The Eg was estimated using the following formula:
(1)
αhv=A(hv−Eg)n
where α, h, ν, A, and Eg present absorption coefficient, Planck’s constant, frequency, a constant, and band gap energy, respectively; n is determined by if the optical transition of semiconductor is direct (n = 1/2) or indirect (n = 2) (Srisasiwimon et al., 2018). The optical transformation of g-C3N4 is indirect (Li et al., 2016a), the value of n is 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of LGQDs at different pH values
ce:section.ce:para: 50 mmol L−1 hydrochloric acid, 50 mmol L−1 sodium citrate, 50 mmol L−1 phosphate, 50 mmol L−1 sodium bicarbonate-sodium hydroxide, and 10 mmol L−1 NaOH were used to prepare different solutions with pH values (2.0–12.0), respectively. A 100 μL LGQDs solution (1.0 mg mL−1) was added into the above solutions (1.0 mL), respectively, and then mixed uniformly. The fluorescence spectra of these solutions were determined, respectively. After that, the relationship between the LGQDs fluorescence intensities and pH values were investigated.

MB was used to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of photocatalysts including ultrasonic g-C3N4 and P-LGQDs/g-C3N4. The photocatalytic reaction of MB with an initial concentration of 20 mg L−1 was conducted in a cylindrical quartz reactor at a 300 W Xe lamp as the light source. Prior to the photocatalytic reaction, the MB and photocatalyst suspension was mixed thoroughly in a dark environment for 0.5 h to reach an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Once the reaction starts, 3.0 mL suspension was taken every 0.5 h, centrifuged, and collected the supernatant for the UV–vis analysis at λ = 664 nm to detect the MB concentration.

A certain amount of acetonitrile, model lignin (2-phenoxy-1-phenylethanol), and photocatalyst (ultrasonic g-C3N4 or P-LGQDs/g-C3N4) were added into a quartz reactor with a volume of 20 mL, and kept magnetically stirring for 0.5 h in dark environment. The photocatalytic reaction was performed at 25 °C for 10 h under the environment of O2 atmosphere and lighting with a 300 W Xe lamp. After the reaction, the products were diluted and analyzed with a gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) (Scion 456-GC-TQ, Bruker, USA) equipped with a flame ionization and TG-170MS GC column. 4'-Methylacetophenone was used as the internal standard. The GC was conducted under the conditions: injection temperature of 250 °C; column temperature of 60 °C for 3 min, temperature ramping to 250 °C for 10 min at 10 °C/min; and detection temperature of 250 °C. The mass spectrometry was scanned in the range of 35–500 m/z. The concentrations of photocatalytic degradation products were calculated as follows:
(2)
C =
C 0
A
A 0
where C is the concentration of degradation products, μg L−1; C0 is the internal standard concentration, μg L−1; A is the peak area of degradation products; A0 is the peak area of internal standard.

LGQDs has a 0.334 nm lattice spacing (Fig. 2 a-1), which is caused by the (002) plane of graphite (Jiang et al., 2018); P-LGQDs demonstrate lattice fringes, which is close to the (020) facet of graphitic carbon with a 0.251 nm lattice spacing (Fig. 2b-1) (Zhang et al., 2019a). The particle size distribution of LGQDs and P-LGQDs is in the range of 2–4 nm and 4–6 nm, respectively (Fig. 2). In FTIR spectra (Fig. 3 a), the presence of hydroxyl and amino groups in LGQDs and P-LGQDs is found from the broad characteristic absorption band at 3500–3000 cm−1 (Zhang et al., 2019a); N and P are found from the absorptions at 1614, 1513, 1430, and 1265 cm−1, attributing to CC/CN stretching vibration, NH asymmetric stretching vibration, CN bending vibrations, and PO stretching vibration, respectively (Wang et al., 2019b; Ye et al., 2019); two distinct peaks 1033 cm−1 and 761 cm−1 are ascribed to PO and PC or POH stretching vibration, respectively (Jiang et al., 2018), confirming that the P element was successfully doped in P-LGQDs during the H3PO4 assisted hydrothermal pretreatment. The UV–vis adsorption peak for both LGQDs and P-LGQDs is at 276 nm (Fig. 3b), which is due to π–π* electronic transition of CC aromatic sp2 domains in graphitic core of GQDs (Liu et al., 2017). Under 365 nm, P-LGQDs aqueous solution shows much stronger bright blue fluorescence than LGQDs; P-LGQDs shows the maximum emission peak at 388 nm upon excitation at 310 nm, while that of LGQDs is at 519 nm when excited at 410 nm (Fig. 3c). P-LGQDs has a much wider optimal excitation spectrum, and the fluorescence intensity of P-LGQDs is much higher than that of LGQDs (Fig. 3d).

The emission wavelength of P-LGQDs gradually red-shifts from 405 to 459 nm with increasing excitation wavelength from 300 to 360 nm, accompanying an intensity reduction; the emission peak of LGQDs is shifted from 480 to 548 nm, when the excitation wavelength is changed from 380 to 440 nm, and the maximum PL intensity is obtained at 410 nm excitation wavelength (Fig. 3e, f), which is mainly resulted from oxygen and/or nitrogen functional groups. These results are found to be consistent with the previous reports, i.e., the band gap of GQDs can be changed by N- and P- doping, leading to GQDs surface defects, thus enhancing excitation dependent emission behavior (Singh et al., 2018). Therefore, phosphoric acid assisted hydrothermal pretreatment can promote the optical and electrical properties of resultant lignin-derived GQDs.

The chemical compositions and structures of P-LGQDs are further characterized by XPS. Elements of C, N, O, and P are found in P-LGQDs (Fig. 4 a). From the spectra of P-LGQDs, the peaks 285.1 eV, 398.1 eV, 532.1 eV, 191.1 eV, and 134.1 eV are attributed to C1s, N1s, O1s, P2s, and P2p, respectively. The high resolution N1s spectrum of P-LGQDs shows two obvious peaks at 397.6 and 400.7 eV, which are associated with pyridinic N and quaternary N, respectively; while two absorption peaks at 400.0 eV and 402.1 eV are found in LGQDs, indicating the dominant form of N–(C3) and NH bonds, respectively (Zhang et al., 2019b) (Fig. 4b, c). The P2p XPS spectrum shows two peaks at 133.4 eV and 134.2 eV, confirming the presence of PC/PN and PO groups, respectively (Huang et al., 2018; Qiao et al., 2018), Fig. 4d, which is in agreement with FTIR analysis. The substitutional defects in diamond sp3 form as a result of P doping; in addition, doping P atoms can generate some new and unexpected properties of GQDs via changing their electronic properties and providing more available active sites (Zhou et al., 2014).

The lignin polymer network consists of hydroxyphenyl, guaiacyl, and syringyl units. The main linkages of these phenylpropane units are alpha- and beta- ether bonds. An effective cleavage of these ether bonds during the hydrothermal treatment is critical to synthesize high-quality carbon materials. The addition of H3PO4 to the hydrothermal process serves this purpose well, because H3PO4-assisted hydrothermal treatment catalyzes the scission/ cleavages of aryl ether bonds, thus facilitating the lignin degradative transformation to carbon materials, with the formation of well-established micro- and meso- porous structures. Another bonus associated with the use of H3PO4, is that the added H3PO4 can dope the carbon materials with P, so that various C to P functionalities will form. These functional groups will provide effective active sites in the carbon materials. LGQDs would contain more P atoms. Thus, the optical, photocatalytic, and electrochemical properties of the resultant LGQDs will be promoted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH responsiveness of LGQDs and P-LGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of P-LGQDs under different pH values were measured (Fig. 5 ). The emission intensities of P-LGQDs are stable in the pH range of 2.0–7.0, which is different from most of GQDs that are only responsive to highly acidic or alkaline environments. Compared with LGQDs (Fig. 5a), the emission intensities of P-LGQDs are relatively stable in the pH 5.0–9.0, but are decreased under strongly acidic or alkaline conditions. The PL emission intensity is reduced at high pH values because of the accumulated negative charge densities and suppression of π–π* and n–π* transitions at high pH (Liu et al., 2019b). The fluorescence spectra exhibit an obvious red-shift at high pH, which might be due to the bonding of protons to emissive sites of P-LGQDs. The main difference between P-LGQDs and LGQDs is attributed to addition of H3PO4 during the hydrothermal pretreatment. Doping of P atoms as a result of H3PO4 assisted hydrothermal pretreatment can bring more negative charge density due to the formation of P-containing groups on carbon materials surface, e.g., phosphoric anhydride, leading to protonation-deprotonation performance especially with the pH changes, consequently leading to improved photoluminescence behaviors (Yang et al., 2018).

Furthermore, the relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH was explored (Fig. 5c, d). 1) P-LGQDs show a good linear relationships between fluorescence intensity and pH in the range of 2.0–7.0 and 9.0–12.0, respectively; the linear regression equations are described as: Y = 164.1X + 5383.1 (R2 = 0.964), Y=-1368.9X + 21351.7 (R2 = 0.960), respectively; The pH-responsive ability of P-LGQDs is caused by the protonation-deprotonation of functional groups such as carboxyl groups and phosphoric anhydride on P-LGQDs surface (Yang et al., 2018). 2) LGQDs also have a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH in the range of 2.0–5.0 and 9.0–12.0, respectively; the linear regression equations are obtained, i.e., Y = 703.6X + 2250.4 (R2 = 0.986), Y=-694.5X + 12403 (R2 = 0.985), respectively. It can be seen that the as-prepared P-LGQDs and LGQDs show a good pH-responsive ability. Due to excellent advantages such as good biocompatibility, limited cytotoxicity, and stable photoluminescence (Yuan et al., 2016), GQDs has been attracting tremendous attention. Therefore, the pH-responsive LGQDs and P-LGQDs would be potentially used in cell biological applications, such as pH sensing, drug delivery and bio-imaging (Ehtesabi et al., 2020).

Fig. 6 a shows the FTIR spectra of P-LGQDs/g-C3N4. The FTIR spectrum of P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 is similar to that of ultrasonic g-C3N4. The isotherms are relatively flat at around P/P0 < 0.55, indicating the existence of micropores (Fig. 6b); an increase within the relative pressure of 0.55–1.00 P/P0, i.e., a type-IV isotherm curve with type H3 hysteresis loops is observed, indicating the presence of mesopores (Mohamed et al., 2019). Interestingly, the hysteresis loop position appears in a wide range of relative pressures, suggesting a wide pore size distribution in P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 (Fig. 6c). The specific surface area of P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 is obviously improved compared to that of ultrasonic g-C3N4, proving that the multi-layer structure of g-C3N4 is loosened, which can facilitate its photocatalytic activity.

P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 shows that a strong emission peak that occurs at about 470 nm under the excitation of 345 nm (Fig. 6d), corresponding to the combination of photoelectron electrons and holes. The PL intensity of P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 is reduced in comparison with that of ultrasonic g-C3N4, suggesting that the integration of P-LGQDs and g-C3N4 can suppress charges recombination. In addition, P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 exhibits an obvious red-shift, i.e., the visible light absorption of P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 is improved, demonstrating that P-LGQDs can extend the visible light absorption range of g-C3N4, which is caused by the quantum confinement effect of P-LGQDs (Fig. 6e). The band gap energy of g-C3N4 is reduced from 2.82 to 2.76 eV after introducing P-LGQDs (Fig. 6f), implying that there may be sub-band energy levels between g-C3N4 and P-LGQDs in the bandgaps of the P-LGQDs/g-C3N4, which is consistent with other’s report (Xia et al., 2015). The results show that these g-C3N4 layers in P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 are peeled from the bulk phase (Fig. 6g1). By comparing with the smooth surface of g-C3N4 (Fig. 6g1-3), some black dots are uniformly distributed on g-C3N4 surface for P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 (Fig. 6h2, h3), proving the presence of P-LGQDs, which is also confirmed by the elemental mapping images of P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 (Fig. 6i). The lattice structure with the spacing of about 0.23 nm is found on P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 surface, which is close to the facet of graphitic carbon. All the above characteristics of P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 will help to promote the charge transfer during the photocatalysis.

The photocatalytic performance of P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 was assessed under 300 W Xe lamp illumination (Fig. 7 a). The adsorption-desorption equilibrium is achieved within 0.5 h in a dark environment. P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 shows excellent photocatalytic degradation ratio towards MB, i.e., 94.6 % vs. 77.9 % (g-C3N4). The reasons for enhancing the photocatalytic performance are mainly caused by the following reasons: 1) the constructed semiconductor heterostructures can effectively suppress the recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, consequently promoting the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs and enhancing the photocatalytic activity; 2) P-LGQDs have good up-conversion ability, which improves the optical absorption intensity of semiconductor heterostructures, and generates more active free radicals, increasing the photocatalytic activity to effectively degrade organic pollutants under visible light irradiation. Rajender et al. (2017) anchored Au nanoparticles on GQDs/ graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), the degradation of MB achieved 86 % under visible light over the irradiation time of 180 min. Xu et al. (2019) constructed g-C3N4/ zinc tetracarboxyphthalocyanine (ZnTcPc)/GQDs, and the removal rate of Rhodamine B exceeded 98.2 %, which was 2 times higher than that of pure g-C3N4. Based on the comparative results, we believe that the present P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 would perform as an efficient photocatalyst.

In addition, P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 as photocatalyst shows excellent photocatalytic degradation of model lignin, 2-phenoxy-1 phenethyl alcohol (Fig. 7b). The conversion rate of model lignin catalyzed by P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 and ultrasonic g-C3N4 are 93.1 % and 80.4 %, respectively (Table 1 ). The photocatalytic reaction generates value-added lignin- derived platform chemicals, including phenyl formate (2), benzaldehyde (3), and benzoic acid (5). The results suggest that the constructed P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunction provides a novel and highly efficient alternative for degrading lignin in pulping and papermaking wastewater, which can not only reduce the effluent load, but also lead to the generation of value-added lignin-based chemicals.

Based on the abovementioned analysis, a possible mechanism of P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 photocatalytic degradation is proposed and illustrated in Fig. 8 . Under simulated solar radiation, ultrasonic g-C3N4 can directly absorb partial light and generate electrons (e−) and holes (h+), the electrons are then excited from valence band (VB) to conduction band (CB). Under the same radiation, P-LGQDs in the heterojunction of P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 absorb visible light and emit light with short wavelength, which can then be absorbed by ultrasonic g-C3N4 and generate e− and h+. Meanwhile, P-LGQDs can serve as electron traps, e− species from the CB of ultrasonic g-C3N4 can be easily transfered to P-LGQDs surface, the holes in the VB react with sacrificial regents, which will help to accelerate the charge separation and effectively inhibit the charge recombination. It can be seen that P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 can not only improve the utilization efficiency of visible light provided by up-conversion of P-LGQDs, but also promote the photo-generated electron-hole pair’s separation, thereby effectively degrading model lignin under irradiation of visible light and promoting its degradation into some other main chemicals.

Holistic value-added utilization is a key strategy for lignin valorization. A phosphorous acid assisted hydrothermal treatment process of lignin was developed to produce lignin-derived GQDs. Phosphorous acid - originated lignin scission breaks down its highly cross-linked structure, with additional benefit that P atoms are doped into resultant carbon materials, thus the optical properties of P-LGQDs are enhanced. The as-prepared P-LGQDs exhibits blue light, a narrow particle size distribution (4–6 nm), a maximum emission peak at 388 nm upon excitation at 310 nm, a good pH-responsive ability, especially in the range of 2.0–7.0 and 9.0–12.0, respectively. The P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 shows excellent photocatalytic degradation ability towards MB and model lignin, i.e., the photocatalytic degradation ratios for MB and model lignin reach 94.6 % and 93.1 %, respectively. In addition, P-LGQDs/g-C3N4 as photocatalyst can potentially generate important lignin- derived platform chemicals such as phenyl formate, benzaldehyde, and benzoic acid. This study provides a value-added lignin utilization strategy in photocatalysis.

Wei Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Chenxi Ning: Investigation, Resources, Validation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Ranran Sang: Investigation, Resources, Validation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Qingxi Hou: Methodology, Supervision. Yonghao Ni: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express their gratitude for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32071715, 31770634), and National Science Foundation for Post-doctoral Scientists of China (2019M651050), and Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control, College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University (2019KF32).
